title: On The Religious Memory Of Mrs. Catherine Thomson, My Christian Friend, Deceased Dec. 16, 1646
content:
When Faith and Love, which parted from thee never,
    Had ripened thy just soul to dwell with God,
    Meekly thou didst resign this earthly load
    Of death, called life, which us from life doth sever.
    Thy works, and alms, and all thy good endeavour,
    Stayed not behind, nor in the grave were trod;
    But, as Faith pointed with her golden rod,
    Followed thee up to joy and bliss for ever.
    Love led them on; and Faith, who knew them best
    Thy handmaids, clad them oer with purple beams
    And azure wings, that up they flew so drest,
    And speak the truth of thee on glorious themes
    Before the Judge; who henceforth bid thee rest,
    And drink thy fill of pure immortal streams.
meaning:
This evocative piece by John Milton, titled "On The Religious Memory Of Mrs. Catherine Thomson, My Christian Friend, Deceased Dec. 16, 1646", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
